Course Title: Using AI for Creative Assets Safely
Category: Media & Entertainment: Publishing, Film, Advertising & Communications, Gaming
Description: Images, music, and video generation risks.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with digital tools and a general interest in creative media production are all you need to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key legal and ethical risks associated with using AI to generate images, music, and video content across publishing, film, advertising, and gaming contexts. You will also be able to apply practical strategies to evaluate AI-generated creative assets safely and explain how to organise your workflows in a way that reduces copyright, consent, and reputational risks.

lessons.lesson title: Using AI for Creative Assets Safely
lessons.Description: This lesson introduces you to the practical risks involved in using AI tools to generate creative assets such as images, music, and video. You will explore how AI-generated content can raise important questions around copyright, ownership, and potential bias, helping you to make more informed decisions in your work. We will look at real-world examples of where things can go wrong, and what steps you can take to protect yourself and your organisation. By the end of the lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of how to use AI creative tools responsibly and confidently, without putting yourself or others at risk.
lessons.content: Images, music, and video generation risks.

slides.subtitle: Images, music and video generation risks.
slides.slidecontent:
• AI creates images, music, video
• Risks exist for every creator
• Copyright and ownership matter
• Mistakes can have real consequences
• Learn to use AI safely

slides.subtitle: When AI learns from existing work
slides.slidecontent:
• AI trained on vast amounts of copyrighted content
• Outputs may accidentally reproduce protected work
• Training data sources rarely disclosed
• Copyright holders can still claim infringement
• Legal liability falls on the user, not the AI company

slides.subtitle: Who owns what AI creates?
slides.slidecontent:
• Ownership of AI outputs legally unclear in most countries
• Platform terms vary widely on usage rights
• Some tools retain licenses to your creations
• Commercial use may require additional permissions
• No universal standard for AI-generated content rights

slides.subtitle: When real people appear without permission
slides.slidecontent:
• AI can generate recognizable faces of real individuals
• No consent obtained from people whose likenesses appear
• Deepfakes create serious reputational risks
• Voice cloning raises similar ethical concerns
• Legal protections for personality rights vary by jurisdiction

slides.subtitle: Technical limitations in AI outputs
slides.slidecontent:
• Training data bias appears in generated content
• Stereotypical representations of gender, race, profession
• Technical errors in faces, hands, text elements
• Factual inaccuracies in generated information
• Brand safety concerns with inappropriate content

slides.subtitle: Essential review before going live
slides.slidecontent:
• Verify no copyrighted styles or elements present
• Check for real faces, logos, or branded content
• Review for factual accuracy and bias
• Confirm compliance with platform AI policies
• Document generation process and prompts used

slides.subtitle: Protecting your business and clients
slides.slidecontent:
• Read platform terms before commercial use
• Check if tools offer indemnification coverage
• Establish clear disclosure policies for AI use
• Train teams on safe AI practices
• Create approval workflows for AI-generated content

slides.subtitle: Practical steps for safe implementation
slides.slidecontent:
• Start with low-risk, internal projects
• Maintain human oversight at every stage
• Keep detailed records of AI tool usage
• Regularly update practices as laws evolve
• Balance innovation with risk management

scripts.script: Welcome to this lesson on using AI for creative assets safely. Over the last few years, AI tools have become remarkably capable at generating images, music, and even video, and that opens up some genuinely exciting possibilities for creators of all kinds. But alongside those possibilities come some real risks that are worth understanding before you dive in. Questions around copyright, ownership, and who actually has the rights to AI generated content are not always straightforward, and getting things wrong can have genuine consequences, whether that is a legal issue, a reputational problem, or simply wasting time and effort on something you cannot actually use. So in this lesson, we are going to walk through those risks in plain, practical terms, and by the end you will have a clear sense of how to approach AI creative tools in a way that is both confident and responsible. There is nothing here that should put you off using these tools. It is simply about using them wisely.

Let's start with one of the most significant risks: copyright infringement through training data. AI tools are trained on massive datasets containing millions of images, songs, and videos, much of which is protected by copyright. When you ask an AI to generate content, it draws patterns from this training data, and sometimes those patterns can be close enough to existing work to cause problems. The challenge is that most AI companies don't disclose exactly what content was used to train their models, so you have no way of knowing if your output might be similar to someone's copyrighted work. And here's the crucial point: even if the AI company trained their model on copyrighted content without permission, the legal liability often falls on you as the end user, not on them. So you need to be particularly careful when generating content that might echo distinctive styles or famous works.

Now let's talk about ownership, because this is where things get really murky. In most countries, the law simply hasn't caught up with AI-generated content, so there's genuine uncertainty about who owns what when an AI creates something. Different platforms have wildly different terms of service too. Some grant you full commercial rights to outputs, others retain licenses to everything you create, and many fall somewhere in between. This matters enormously if you're doing client work or creating content for commercial use. You could find yourself in a situation where you can't actually deliver what you promised because the ownership isn't clear. Always read the fine print of whatever tool you're using, and if you're not certain about the rights situation, get legal advice before proceeding with anything commercially important.

Another major concern is consent and identity rights. AI image and video generators can produce remarkably realistic faces, and sometimes these faces belong to real people who never gave permission for their likeness to be used. This isn't just an ethical issue, it's a legal one too. Many jurisdictions have strong personality rights laws that protect people from unauthorized use of their image or voice. The rise of deepfake technology makes this even more serious, because you can now generate convincing video of real people saying or doing things they never actually did. This creates enormous reputational risks, not just for the people whose likenesses are used without consent, but for you and your organization if you publish this kind of content. Voice cloning raises similar concerns, and the legal landscape around these technologies is evolving rapidly.

Beyond legal risks, there are also quality and bias issues that can seriously damage your reputation. Because AI models learn from human-created content, they inevitably absorb the biases present in that training data. This means AI-generated content often reflects stereotypical or outdated representations of different groups of people. You might ask for a doctor and consistently get images of men, or ask for a CEO and see only certain ethnicities represented. There are technical limitations too. AI-generated images famously struggle with hands, often producing extra fingers or unnatural poses. Text within images is frequently garbled, and factual information can be completely wrong despite sounding convincing. All of these issues can undermine your credibility if they make it into published content, so careful human review is essential.

So how do you protect yourself before publishing AI-generated content? Develop a systematic review process. First, check carefully for any copyrighted elements, especially if you've asked the AI to work in a particular style or reference specific works. Look for real faces, recognizable logos, or branded content that could cause legal problems. Review the content for factual accuracy and bias, particularly if it depicts people or makes any kind of informational claims. You also need to make sure you're complying with your platform's policies on AI-generated content, because these vary widely and change frequently. Finally, keep detailed records of how content was generated, including the prompts you used and which tool created it. This documentation can be crucial if questions arise later about the content's origins.

From an organizational perspective, you need to think strategically about managing AI risks. Before using any tool commercially, thoroughly review their terms of service and understand exactly what rights you're getting. Some platforms offer indemnification coverage, meaning they'll defend you if copyright claims arise, but many don't. Establish clear policies about when and how AI use should be disclosed, because transparency requirements vary by industry and client. Make sure your team is properly trained on these risks and knows how to evaluate AI outputs safely. Consider implementing approval workflows for AI-generated content, especially for client work or public-facing materials. The goal isn't to avoid AI entirely, but to use it in a way that protects your business and your clients from unnecessary legal and reputational exposure.

Finally, let's talk about building sustainable, responsible AI workflows. The key is to start small and build confidence gradually. Begin with low-risk, internal projects where you can experiment and learn without exposing your organization to significant problems. Always maintain human oversight throughout the process - AI should augment human creativity and judgment, not replace it. Keep detailed records of your AI usage, including which tools you used, when, and for what purpose. This documentation becomes increasingly important as regulations develop. Remember that this is a rapidly evolving field, so stay informed about changing laws, platform policies, and industry best practices. The goal is to find the right balance between innovation and risk management, allowing you to harness the creative power of AI while protecting yourself and others from potential pitfalls.

Who is this for: Creative professionals, content creators, graphic designers, marketing coordinators, social media managers, video editors, copywriters, brand managers, advertising executives, game developers, publishers
Team or Dept this is for: Marketing, Creative Services, Brand Management, Communications, Content Development, Legal, Compliance
